*Free Electives  : The code for free elective courses in MMU starts with the alphabet ‘F’. Free elective courses maybe within the students’ general area of study or totally outside of students’ area of study depending on the specific 
programme’s requirement but the credits from these courses contribute to the total graduating credit.

Free elective courses maybe within the students’ general area of study or totally outside of students’ area of study depending on the specific 
programme’s requirement but the credits from these courses contribute to the total graduating credit. Example : Free Elective Requirement for Bachelor of Multimedia (Hons ) Interface Design 
2 Free Elective courses or a total of 5 Credit Hours (CH) which can be taken in any of the following combinations:
1. All 2 courses totalling 5 credit hours taken from elective courses offered by FCM (3 CH + 2 CH); or
2. A combination of courses offered by FCM and other faculties. If the total credit hours of the elective courses are 6 CH, there is an additional charge for the extra 1 CH that exceed the required total credit hours in your programme 
structure.

source_file: LDCW6123 — Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.pdf
path: ./data/global_docs\master_raw_sources\course_pdfs\LDCW6123 — Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.pdf
text:
1
2Describe measures for digital safety and solutions for common digital problems in a modern workplace environment. Demonstrate ability to work with digital information collaboratively through collaboration and communication tools. Build a program collaboratively with digital content.

source_file: LDCW6123 — Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.pdf
path: ./data/global_docs\master_raw_sources\course_pdfs\LDCW6123 — Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.pdf
text:
Demonstrate ability to work with digital information collaboratively through collaboration and communication tools. Build a program collaboratively with digital content. Course Content Outline
Information and Data Literacy, Browsing, Searching and Filtering Data, Information, and digital content, Evaluating Data, Information and Digital Content, Managing 
Data, Information and Digital ContentDigital Competence and Its Framework, Information 
and Data Literacy, Communication and collaboration,  
Digital Content Creation, Safety in Digital World, 
Problem Solving in Digital WorldCOURSE INFORMATION
Name of Course Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers
Course Code LDCW6123
Credit Hours 3
Pre-Requisite None
Contact Hours 41 hours 
Assessment Assignment 1 = 30%
Assignment 2 = 30%
Project = 40% Objective of the course in the programme:
The course aims to introduce the knowledge and skills of the digital competence and its framework elements.

text:

Course Code LDCW6123

Project = 40% Objective of the course in the programme:
The course aims to introduce the knowledge and skills of the digital competence and its framework elements. It also exposes learners to the emergence of digital competence as a new 
approach and skills needed to manage daily life tasks and industry.

CLO3:Menjelaskan konsep etika dan peradaban dalam konteks penghayatannya mengikut acuan Malaysia. Menganalisis sistem, tahap perkembangan, kemajuan sosial dan kebudayaan merentas etnik. Membincangkan isu kontemporari berkaitan ekonomi, politik, sosial, budaya dan alam sekitar daripada perspektif etika dan peradaban. Course Content Outline
Pengenalan Penghayatan Etika dan Peradaban Dalam Acuan Malaysia 
- Pengenalan Etika dan Peradaban. 
- Susur masa Etika dan Peradaban berdasarkan 
Prakolonial, Kolonial dan Pascakolonial.
- Kerangka menganalisis Etika dan Peradaban acuan Malaysia melalui sejarah, struktur sosial dan biografi.
- Penghayatan Etika dan Peradaban mengikut peringkat individu, keluarga, komuniti, dan masyarakat.
- Penghayatan Etika dan Peradaban mengikut konteks masyarakat, negara dan pasaran.Contact Hours

1Corruption and Anti-Corruption
Definition of corruption/anti -corruption in general,
The country's position in the Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI), Definition of integrity, Areas of employment at high risk of corruption, Cultivate integrity, Government 
initiatives to enhance the integrity of the civil serviceCourse Content OutlineCLO1:
CLO2:Objective of the course in the programme:
The course aims to spread the importance of integrity and anti-corruption practices among the young generation before they enter the workforce. It provides knowledge and 
awareness so that the young generation can identify the act of corruption and the proper actions to counter it. Course Learning Outcomes (CLO)
Apply the concept of integrity and anti-corruption in daily activities. Demonstrate ability to leadand work collaboratively.

Subject Code:	ARC6123
Objective:	To provide a practical introduction to microcontrollers and microprocessors, assembly language programming techniques, interface hardware design, and microcontroller and microprocessor system design considerations. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	45 hours (lectures, tutorials & laboratory experiments)
Assessment:	Lab: 10%
Test/Quiz: 10%
Tutorial/Assignment: 30%
Final Examination: 50%
Laboratory:	1. Familiarization with the trainer & programming fundamentals.
2.

Familiarization with the trainer & programming fundamentals.
2. Construction and programming of a basic electronic piano
Course Contents:
Basics of Microcontroller and Microprocessor
A basic microprocessor system: the CPU, memory, I/O, and buses subsystems, basic operation of a microprocessor system: fetch and execute cycle, overview of different generations of microprocessor, highlights of architectural and technological advancement of contemporary microprocessors, differences between microcontroller and microprocessor, the architecture of typical microcontrollers, and their features. The MCS-51 Microcontroller
Features of the 8051 – the core of MCS-51 family, block diagram and definitions of the pin of the 8051, I/O port structure, memory organisation: general purpose RAM, bit addressable RAM, register bank, special function registers, external memory, memory space mapping and decoding, bus control signals timing, a typical 8051 microcontroller based system.

The MCS-51 Microcontroller
Features of the 8051 – the core of MCS-51 family, block diagram and definitions of the pin of the 8051, I/O port structure, memory organisation: general purpose RAM, bit addressable RAM, register bank, special function registers, external memory, memory space mapping and decoding, bus control signals timing, a typical 8051 microcontroller based system. MCS-51 Instruction Set and Assembly Language Programming
Addressing modes, the MCS-51 instruction set and typical examples, assembly language format, assembler directives, operation of assemblers and linkers, programming examples. On-chip Peripheral Devices and Interrupt System
I/O ports: operations and uses of port 0, port 1, port 2, port 3, Timers: their operations, programming, and applications, serial port: operations and programming, typical applications.

On-chip Peripheral Devices and Interrupt System
I/O ports: operations and uses of port 0, port 1, port 2, port 3, Timers: their operations, programming, and applications, serial port: operations and programming, typical applications. Organisation of the interrupt system, interrupt vectors, interrupt timings, serial port interrupts, and external interrupts, implementation of single and multiple interrupts. Interfacing Examples
Keypad, seven-segment LED display, LCD display, ADC and DAC chips, and input / output port expansion, description and uses of hardware development tools.

Interfacing Examples
Keypad, seven-segment LED display, LCD display, ADC and DAC chips, and input / output port expansion, description and uses of hardware development tools. Course Learning Outcomes:

Course Learning Outcomes	Level of Emphasis
CLO1 – Explain the fundamental features and operations of contemporary microcontrollers and microprocessors. (cognitive – understanding, level 2)	–
CLO2 – Explain the hardware architecture of a typical 8051 microcontroller. (cognitive – understanding, level 2)	–
CLO3 – Analyse the MCS-51 Instruction Set. (cognitive – analysing, level 4)	–
CLO4 – Develop assembly language programs for 8051 based embedded systems. (cognitive – creating, level 6)	–

Course Contents:
Introduction
Components of machine vision system; application of machine vision system; properties of cameras and lens, lighting systems; image generation, 2-D representations; image geometry; image modalities; level of processing: point, local, global, object. Fundamental of Image Processing
Elements of visual perception; image sensing and acquisition; image sampling and quantization; digital image representation and resolution; binary image processing; gray-level image processing, colour image processing; arithmetic operations; histogram operations; morphological operations; geometrical transformations.

Fundamental of Image Processing
Elements of visual perception; image sensing and acquisition; image sampling and quantization; digital image representation and resolution; binary image processing; gray-level image processing, colour image processing; arithmetic operations; histogram operations; morphological operations; geometrical transformations. Image Segmentation
Edge-based segmentation: edge detection, gradient operator, Laplacian operator, edge linking and boundary detection, Hough transform; Region-based segmentation: thresholding, region growing, region splitting & merging; feature clustering; watershed segmentation; post-processing.

Image Segmentation
Edge-based segmentation: edge detection, gradient operator, Laplacian operator, edge linking and boundary detection, Hough transform; Region-based segmentation: thresholding, region growing, region splitting & merging; feature clustering; watershed segmentation; post-processing. Image Representation and Recognition
Image representation: representation schemes, low-level vision: colour, texture and shape; shape from texture and shape from shading, regional descriptors, boundary descriptors; Image recognition: image analysis, patterns and pattern classes, theory of pattern recognition, determination and analysis of object signature, decision-theoretic method, knowledge interpretation.

Image Representation and Recognition
Image representation: representation schemes, low-level vision: colour, texture and shape; shape from texture and shape from shading, regional descriptors, boundary descriptors; Image recognition: image analysis, patterns and pattern classes, theory of pattern recognition, determination and analysis of object signature, decision-theoretic method, knowledge interpretation. Image Representation and Recognition
Video Processing
Video formation and representation: analog and digital video, video capture and display, spatio-temporal video sampling and sampling rate conversion; motion field and optical flow; motion estimation and compensation; object tracking; robot vision; stereo vision. Machine Vision Hardware
Embedded machine vision hardware; Arduino/Raspberry Pi platform with camera; OpenCV software library.

Machine Vision Hardware
Embedded machine vision hardware; Arduino/Raspberry Pi platform with camera; OpenCV software library. Course Learning Outcomes:

Course Contents:
Fundamentals of Semiconductor Devices
Nature of Atom, Energy-band in semiconductors, Electrons and holes in an intrinsic semiconductor, P and N doping, impurity level, Transport phenomena in semiconductor. P-N Junction Characteristics and Diode Models

Half-wave rectifier, full-wave rectifier, RC filters, clipping circuits, Pulse shaping, clamping circuits, voltage-doubler.

Graphical analysis of CE configuration, h-parameter, Hybrid-pi and simplified hybrid-pi and the re models; Input resistance, Output resistance, Voltage gain, current gain; CE, CC and CB amplifiers, Cascading transistor amplifiers. Course Learning Outcomes:

Digital Design
Subject Code:	ARE6123
Objective:	To provide a basic understanding of the basics of logic circuits and their applications in digital system. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	47 hours (lectures, tutorials & laboratory experiments)
Assessment:	Lab: 10%
Assignment: 15%
Test/Quiz: 15%
Final Examination: 60%
Laboratory:	1. DL1: Logic Gates And Their Applications
2. DL2: Flip-flop And Their Applications
Course Contents:
Introduction
Digital vs. analog systems. Digital system design hierarchy. Organization of a stored program digital computer. Logic devices : TTL and CMOS families. Number Systems and Codes
Positional notation, number systems, binary arithmetic, octal arithmetic, hexadecimal arithmetic. Base conversions. Signed number representation, computer codes. Algebraic Methods for the Analysis and Synthesis of Logic Circuits
Fundamentals of Boolean algebra. Basic postulates: fundamental theorems of, Boolean algebra, switching functions, truth tables.

Algebraic Methods for the Analysis and Synthesis of Logic Circuits
Fundamentals of Boolean algebra. Basic postulates: fundamental theorems of, Boolean algebra, switching functions, truth tables. Algebraic forms of switching functions. Derivation of canonical forms. Switching circuits. Electronic logic gates, basic functional components. Analysis of combinational circuits. Synthesis of combinational logic Circuits. AND-OR and NAND networks, OR-AND and NOR networks. Two-level circuits. AND-OR-inverter circuits. Computer-aided design of logic circuits. Simplification of Switching Functions
Characteristics of minimization methods. Karnaugh maps. K-maps of four or more variables. Plotting functions in canonical form on the K-map. Simplification of switching functions using K-maps. Algorithms for deriving minimal SOP forms from K-maps. POS form using K-maps. Algorithms for deriving minimal POS forms from K-maps. Quine-McCluskey tabular minimization method.

Algorithms for deriving minimal SOP forms from K-maps. POS form using K-maps. Algorithms for deriving minimal POS forms from K-maps. Quine-McCluskey tabular minimization method. Computer-aided minimization of switching functions. Algebraic methods for determining prime implicants. Introduction to Sequential Logic
Models for sequential circuits. Block diagram representation. State tables and diagrams. Memory devices. Latches: set-reset latch, gated SR latch, delay latch. Flip-flops: master-slave SR flip-flops, master-slave D flip-flops, master-slave JK flip-flops, edge-triggered D flip-flops, edge-triggered JK flip-flops, T flip-flops. Other memory devices. Timing circuits. Modular Combinational Logic
Modular Design. Decoders. Decoder Circuit Structures. Implementing Logic Functions Using Decoder. Encoder Circuit Structures. Multiplexers/Data. Selectors. Multiplexer Circuit Structures. Applications of Multiplexers. Demultiplexers/Data Distributors. Binary Arithmetic Elements.

Encoder Circuit Structures. Multiplexers/Data. Selectors. Multiplexer Circuit Structures. Applications of Multiplexers. Demultiplexers/Data Distributors. Binary Arithmetic Elements. Binary Adder Circuits. Binary Subtraction Circuits. Arithmetic Overflow Detection. Comparators. Design Example: A Computer Arithmetic Logic Unit. Computer-aided Design of Modular Systems.

Binary Adder Circuits. Binary Subtraction Circuits. Arithmetic Overflow Detection. Comparators. Design Example: A Computer Arithmetic Logic Unit. Computer-aided Design of Modular Systems. Course Learning Outcomes:
At the completion of the subject, students should be able to perform the following tasks:
Course Learning Outcomes	Level of Emphasis
CLO1 – Distinguish the analog and digital systems, apply positional notations, number systems and computer codes in digital systems. (cognitive – understanding, level 2)	–
CLO2 – Use Boolean algebra, the Karnaugh maps and Quine-McCluskey tabular methods to simplify and design logic circuits. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO3 – Apply the concepts of sequential logic and memory devices in digital systems. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO4 – Design modular combinational circuits using encoders, decoders, multiplexers and demultiplexers. (cognitive -creating, level 6)	–

Course Contents:
Operational Amplifier Fundamentals
Ideal Op-Amp, Virtual Ground Concept, Basic Op-Amp Circuits, Op-Amp applications: current-to-voltage converters, voltage-to-current converters, Difference amplifier, Instrumentation amplifier, integrator, differentiator. Active Filters
Biquadratic Transfer Functions, Filter types, Standard Responses, Sallen-Key Networks, Frequency and Impedance Scaling, Gain Adjustments, Frequency Transformations, State-variable configurations.

Active Filters
Biquadratic Transfer Functions, Filter types, Standard Responses, Sallen-Key Networks, Frequency and Impedance Scaling, Gain Adjustments, Frequency Transformations, State-variable configurations. Practical Op-Amp Limitations
Simplified circuit diagram of op-amp input stage, input bias and offset current, input offset voltage, voltage drift, power supply rejection ratio, common-mode rejection ratio, offset error compensation, frequency response, transient response, slew rate, input and output impedances, noise, stability, gain and phase margins, frequency compensation. OP-amp Voltage Regulators
Voltage regulation, OP-amp based: Basic Series regulator, Short circuit (or) Overload Protection, regular with Fold-back current limiting, Basic shunt regulator. Basic Switching regulator, Step-up configuration and step-down configuration.

Basic Switching regulator, Step-up configuration and step-down configuration. Oscillators
General principles of oscillation, Barkhausen Criterion, Phase-Shift Oscillator, Wien-Bridge Oscillator, LC Oscillators, Crystal Oscillators, Non Sinusoidal waveform generator. Nonlinear Circuit Applications
Comparators, Schmitt Triggers, Precision Rectifiers, Peak Detectors, Log/Antilog Amplifiers, Analog Multipliers.

Comparators, Schmitt Triggers, Precision Rectifiers, Peak Detectors, Log/Antilog Amplifiers, Analog Multipliers. Course Learning Outcomes:

Electrical Circuits
Subject Code:	ARL6113
Objective:	To provide a basic understanding of the laws of circuit theory and the behaviour of circuits during steady-state and transient conditions. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	46 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)
Assessment:	Test/Quiz: 20%
Assignment: 20%

Final Examination: 50%
Laboratory:
CK1: Thevenin’s Theorem and Superposition Theorem
CK2: AC Circuits
Course Contents:
Circuit Elements and Signal Waveforms
Electric quantities: charges, voltage, current, power, energy. Voltage and current sources, resistor, inductor and capacitor. Signal waveforms: d.c., step, impulse, square pulse, sinusoidal, triangular, exponential. General description of signals: time constant, rms value, duty cycle, crest factor, form factor. Basic Laws of Electric Circuit
Ohm’s Law, Kirchhoff’s current and voltage Laws, Thevenin and Norton equivalents, superposition, reciprocity, maximum power transfer theorem.

Basic Laws of Electric Circuit
Ohm’s Law, Kirchhoff’s current and voltage Laws, Thevenin and Norton equivalents, superposition, reciprocity, maximum power transfer theorem. Network Analysis
Elementary network topology, network constraints, network equilibrium equations. Nodal and mesh network analysis. Sinusoidal Steady State Analysis
Effective alternating current. a.c. behavior in R, L and C elements. Phasor analysis with complex algebra, Two terminal networks – impedance, admittance and their real and imaginary parts. Resonance: series and parallel resonance, half power points, bandwidth, Q-factors. Power: instantaneous, average, power factor, active, reactive, complex, apparent. Transient Analysis
Analysis of first order LR and RC circuits subjected to excitation of d.c., square pulse, sinusoidal sources and exponential sources. Interpretation of complementary function and particular integral. Analysis of second order RLC circuit subjected to step input and sinusoidal input.

Interpretation of complementary function and particular integral. Analysis of second order RLC circuit subjected to step input and sinusoidal input. Course Learning Outcomes:

CLO3 – Compute the impedance, resonance and complex power of sinusoidal circuits. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	High
CLO4 – Analyse the transient response of RL, RC and RLC circuits. (cognitive – analysing, level 4)	High
CLO5 – Construct a circuit by using circuit theory experiment board to verify the law of electric circuit and phasor relationship. (psychomotor – precision, level 3)	Low

Subject Code:	ARL6123
Objective:	To introduce the basic concepts and working principles of electrical machines and transformers as well as electrical power systems and their protection methods. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3

Course Contents:
Introduction to machinery principles
Introduction to electrical machines and transformers. Rotational motion, Newton’s law, and power relationships.

Rotational motion, Newton’s law, and power relationships. Magnetic field, Faraday’s law, induced voltage on a conductor moving in a magnetic field, production of force on a wire in a magnetic field. Three-phase circuits
Balanced three-phase voltages. Balanced three-phase systems. Relation between line and phase quantities. Analysis of balanced three-phase circuits. Y-Delta transformation. Real power, reactive power, and complex power calculations. Introduction to different types of protection elements (CB, Relay etc….). Importance of safety and grounding. Transformers
Single-phase transformers: Construction and principle of operation of real transformer, equivalent circuit, phasor diagram, efficiency and voltage regulation, short and open circuit tests.Three-phase transformers: Construction and connections. Special transformers: autotransformer, instrument transformer. D.C.

Special transformers: autotransformer, instrument transformer. D.C. Machines
Basic principles of operation and construction of DC machines for practical robotics applications, Emf equation and principle of commutation. Types of DC generators and DC motors. DC motors: Torque-speed relationship for robot motion, characteristics of shunt, series and compound motors. Introduction to the servo motor. Starting and speed control. Losses and efficiencies of DC motors in robotic systems. AC machines (Induction Motors & Synchronous Motors)
Three-phase Induction Motors
Principle of operation. The basic construction of three-phase induction motors. Slip equation. Equivalent circuit. Determination of equivalent circuit parameters. Torque-speed characteristics. Starting methods. Synchronous Motor
Principle of operation. Basic construction. Equivalent circuit. Phasor Diagram, Method of Starting. Power flow. Power developed.

Torque-speed characteristics. Starting methods. Synchronous Motor
Principle of operation. Basic construction. Equivalent circuit. Phasor Diagram, Method of Starting. Power flow. Power developed. Different torques

Subject Code:	ARL6143
Objective:	To develop skills for circuit and signal analysis as well as filter design. Pre-Requisite:	None

Introduction to Signals
Concept of signals.

Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	45 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)
Assessment:	Test: 15%
Assignment: 15%
Lab Assessment: 10%
Final exam: 60%
Laboratory:	–
Course Contents:
Introduction to Signals
Concept of signals. Signal classifications: continuous vs. discrete, deterministic vs. stochastic, even vs. odd, periodic vs. aperiodic, energy vs. power. Mathematical models of continuous-time signals: sinusoidal, exponential, unit step, unit pulse and unit impulse. Signal approximation by impulse functions. Linear convolution (i.e. convolution integral, graphical evaluation and numerical evaluation) and its properties. Discrete-time signal models: unit step and unit delta. Discrete-time convolution (i.e. convolution sum, graphical evaluation and convolution table) and its properties. Network Topology
Introduction to network topology and graph terminologies. Standard branch representation and its matrix form.

Network Topology
Introduction to network topology and graph terminologies. Standard branch representation and its matrix form. Circuit analysis of topological networks using matrix by node analysis, mesh analysis, fundamental cut-set analysis and fundamental loop analysis.tion by impulse functions. Linear convolution (i.e. convolution integral, graphical evaluation and numerical evaluation) and its properties. Discrete-time signal models: unit step and unit delta. Discrete-time convolution (i.e. convolution sum, graphical evaluation and convolution table) and its properties. Circuit Analysis using Laplace Transform
Introduction to Laplace transform (s-domain) and its properties. Inverse Laplace transform and method of partial fraction. Circuit analysis using Laplace transform: resistor, capacitor and inductor models. Applications of Laplace transform to first and second order networks. Concept of poles, zeros and transfer function. Frequency response in s-domain and Bode diagrams.

Applications of Laplace transform to first and second order networks. Concept of poles, zeros and transfer function. Frequency response in s-domain and Bode diagrams. State Variable Analysis of RLC Networks
Concepts of state and state variables. Formulation of state and output equations of simple RLC networks using state-transition matrix. Transfer function of state and output equations in s-domain. Two-Port Networks
Concepts of one- and two-port networks. Two-port network parameters (matrix form) and circuit models: z-, y-, h-, g-, a- and b-parameters. Relationship between two-port network parameters. Reciprocity and symmetric properties of two-port networks. Equivalence of two-port networks: T-, p- and lattice networks. Network interconnections: series, parallel, series-parallel, parallel-series and cascade. Analysis of terminated two-port networks: current gain, input impedance, voltage gain and output impedance.

Network interconnections: series, parallel, series-parallel, parallel-series and cascade. Analysis of terminated two-port networks: current gain, input impedance, voltage gain and output impedance. Network Synthesis of Passive Elements
Introduction to network synthesis. Hurtwitz polynomial. Positive real function. Synthesis of one-port network (i.e. RC, RL, LC) using Foster and Cauer methods. Properties of immittance functions of RC, RL and LC networks. Passive Filters
Introduction to ideal filters and its design elements: lowpass, highpass, bandpass and bandstop. Practical filter designs using passive elements: resistor, capacitor and inductor. Filter transformations (i.e. lowpass, highpass and bandpass) using frequency and impedance denormalizations. Butterworth and Chebyshev filter approximations.

Filter transformations (i.e. lowpass, highpass and bandpass) using frequency and impedance denormalizations. Butterworth and Chebyshev filter approximations. Course Learning Outcomes:

Course Learning Outcomes	Level of Emphasis
CLO1 – Perform signal classification, signal modelling and convolution. (cognitive – understanding, level 2)	–
CLO2 – Analyse electric circuits using network graphs. (cognitive – analyzing, level 4)	–
CLO3 – Compute network parameters for two-port networks. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO4 – Solve for the time and frequency behaviour of electric circuits using Laplace Transform and state variable analysis. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO5 – Construct one-port networks and filters. (psychomotor – guided response, level 3)	–

Feedback control
Subject Code:	ARL6153
Objective:	To introduce time domain, root locus and frequency domain methods for analysis and design of feedback control systems. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	45 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Course Contents:
Introduction to Control Systems
Definition of open-loop and closed-loop control systems. Examples of control systems. Review of differential equations, Laplace transform and inverse Laplace transform. System Modelling
Definition of transfer functions. Modeling of electrical, mechanical and electromechanical systems. Block diagram reduction using signal flow graph and block diagram reduction techniques. Time Domain Analysis of Control Systems
Definitions of transient response parameters. Analysis of second-order system as prototype. Evaluation of stability using Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion.

Time Domain Analysis of Control Systems
Definitions of transient response parameters. Analysis of second-order system as prototype. Evaluation of stability using Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. Classification of systems based on steady-state error characteristics. Root Locus Method
Definition of root locus, properties of root locus, sketching of root locus plots. Effect of open-loop zeros and poles. Root locus design concepts. Frequency Domain Analysis
Definitions of transient response parameters. Analysis of second-order system as prototype. Evaluation of stability using Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. Classification of systems based on steady-state error characteristics
Compensation Techniques
Compensation techniques: lag, lead and lag-lead compensation, PD, PI and PID controllers. Cascade compensation based on root locus method. Introduction to feedback compensation.

Cascade compensation based on root locus method. Introduction to feedback compensation. Course Learning Outcomes:

CLO3 – Analyse properties of control systems using frequency domain methods. (cognitive – analyzing, level 4)	–
CLO4 – Design compensators for control systems using strategic thinking. (cognitive – creating, level 6)	–

Course Contents:
Calculus of One Variable
Elementary functions and their inverses, graphs. Limits: intuitive approach and computation, end behaviour of a function. Continuity and differentiability. Techniques of differentiation: product, quotient and chain rules. Implicit differentiation. L’Hôpital’s Rule.Applications of differentiation. Maximum and minimum values. Techniques of Integration. Applications of integration. Sequences of real numbers, Infinite series. Taylor’s and Maclaurin series expansion. Fourier Series
Periodic functions, Fourier series of 2L-periodic functions, convergence and sum of Fourier series, even and odd functions. Half range sine and cosine series expansions. Complex form of Fourier series. Applications of Fourier series in engineering problems.

Half range sine and cosine series expansions. Complex form of Fourier series. Applications of Fourier series in engineering problems. Complex Numbers Functions and Vector Algebra
Graphical representation and addition of complex numbers. Polar and exponential forms of a complex number. DeMoivre’s theorem, powers and nth-roots of complex numbers. Euler formula. Elementary functions of a complex variable. Dot product and cross product, triple products and their geometrical applications. Equations for a line or a plane. Vectors in Rn space,addition and scalar multiplication. Probability
Probability space. Probability theory. Conditional probability and independence, concept of a random variable, discrete and continuous distributions, mean and variance. Examples involving experimental measurement and reliability.

Conditional probability and independence, concept of a random variable, discrete and continuous distributions, mean and variance. Examples involving experimental measurement and reliability. Course Learning Outcomes:

Course Learning Outcomes	Level of Emphasis

CLO2 – Perform basic vector operations and calculate the powers and roots of complex numbers. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO3 – Construct the power series and the Fourier series representations of functions. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO4 – Determine the probability, mean and variance for various distributions in discrete and continuous random variables. (cognitive – evaluating, level 5)	–

Subject Code:	ARM6123

Course Contents:
Ordinary Differential Equations
Introduction and characteristics of a differential equation: definition, degree, order, linearity, homogeneity, concept of solution. First order equations, separable variables, exact equations, integrating factors, linear equations with applications. Reduction of special case of non-linear first order ODE (Bernoulli equation) to linear form.

Reduction of special case of non-linear first order ODE (Bernoulli equation) to linear form. Second order linear differential equations with constant coefficients, Homogeneous equations with applications. Non-homogeneous equations: complementary functions, particular integrals, method of undetermined coefficients. Higher order (homogeneous and non-homogeneous) linear differential equations with constant coefficients. Elementary power series methods.
al Examination: 60%
Partial Differentiation
Functions of several variables: graphs of functions of two variables, level curves and surfaces.Limits, continuity and partial derivatives. Higher-order partial derivatives, equality of mixed partials.Differentiability, total differentials, approximations using differentials and their applications to engineering problems.Chain rule. Implicit differentiation. Extremum problems, without and with constraints, Lagrange multipliers, global extremum. Applications of extremum problems.

Implicit differentiation. Extremum problems, without and with constraints, Lagrange multipliers, global extremum. Applications of extremum problems. Integral Transform
Derivation of transforms and inverses (Fourier and Laplace). Properties of transforms. Applications of these transforms in boundary and initial value problems -transform and its application to solve finite difference equations. Partial Differential Equations
asic concepts of partial differential equations. Classification of second order linear partial differential equation into basic types. The principle of linear superposition. The wave, diffusion and Laplace equations. Boundary and initial-value problems. D’Alembert’s solution for wave equation. Method of separation of variables. Solving inhomogeneous PDE’s or PDE’s with inhomogeneous boundary conditions.

Boundary and initial-value problems. D’Alembert’s solution for wave equation. Method of separation of variables. Solving inhomogeneous PDE’s or PDE’s with inhomogeneous boundary conditions. Course Learning Outcomes:

Objective:	This course introduces students to the mathematical concepts and analysis methods of linear algebra, numerical methods and optimization for solving practical probems in science and engineering.

Linear Algebra and Numerical Methods
Subject Code:	ARM6133
Objective:	This course introduces students to the mathematical concepts and analysis methods of linear algebra, numerical methods and optimization for solving practical probems in science and engineering. In addition, students will be exposed to scientific computing software that are used to generate solutions, particularly solutions to large-scale problems. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	48 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)
Assessment:	Test 1: 15%
Test 2: 10%
Quiz: 5%
Assignment: 10%
Final exam: 60%
Laboratory:	–
Course Contents:
Linear Algebra
Matrices, matrix operations. Determinants and inverse. Solution of system of linear equations by matrices. Gaussian elimination. Rank of a matrix. Elementary matrices. Matrix inverse by elementary matrices. Characteristic polynomial, characteristic equation, eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Diagonalization of matrices.

Rank of a matrix. Elementary matrices. Matrix inverse by elementary matrices. Characteristic polynomial, characteristic equation, eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Diagonalization of matrices. Using scientific computing software in linear algebra. Numerical Methods
Interpolation and its applications. Numerical differentiation and integration. Newton Raphson method for roots of equations. Numerical solutions of ordinary differential equations. Numerical methods in solving system of linear equations. Scientific computing software for numerical methods. Optimization
Formulation of decision problems as mathematical models. Linear programming: objective function, constraints, slack variable. Solution using graphical method and simplex technique. Dual and primal problems. Post-optimal analysis. Combinatorial problems: shortest path and shortest spanning trees problems.

Solution using graphical method and simplex technique. Dual and primal problems. Post-optimal analysis. Combinatorial problems: shortest path and shortest spanning trees problems. Course Learning Outcomes:

Course Learning Outcomes	Level of Emphasis
CLO1 – Use various methods in linear algebra to solve systems of algebraic equations. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO2 -Apply numerical methods to solve mathematical problems including interpolation, differentiation, integration, root finding, ordinary differential equations and systems of linear equations. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO3 – Determine the optimal solutions to the optimization problems using appropriate methods. (cognitive – evaluating, level 5)	–

Course Contents:
Introduction
Kinematics and kinetics. Mechanisms and machines. The design process. Human factors engineering. Kinematics Fundamentals
Types of motion. Links, joints and kinematic chains. Degrees of freedom or mobility. Mechanism and structures. Isomers. Linkage transformation. Intermittent motion. Linkage Synthesis
Function, path and motion generation. Dimensional synthesis. Quick return mechanisms. Springs. Straight-line mechanisms. Other useful linkages. Engineering Materials
Static strength. Plastic deformation. Strength and cold work. Hardness. Temperature effects. Alloy steels.

Springs. Straight-line mechanisms. Other useful linkages. Engineering Materials
Static strength. Plastic deformation. Strength and cold work. Hardness. Temperature effects. Alloy steels. Corrosion resistant steels. Nonferrous metals. Plastics. Screws, Fasteners and Nonpermanent Joints Thread standards and definitions
Threaded fasteners. Set screws. Pins and Keys. Joints – fastener and member stiffness. Bearings, Couplings and Lubrication
Bearing types. Bearing load and lifespan. Selection of ball and cylindrical roller bearings. Lubrication for bearing. Friction-contact axial clutches. Band-type clutches. Cone clutches.

Subject Code:	ARR6133
Objective:	To acquire technical knowledge in the field of actuators and sensors, and to design programmable logic controllers used in various industrial applications. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	46 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)
Assessment:	Test: 10%
Assignment: 30%
Lab Assessment: 20%
Final exam: 40%
Laboratory:	–\
Course Contents:
Basic Principle of Actuators and Sensors
Principle of drive operation. Definition of actuator, sensor, controller and load. Classification of actuators and sensors, and their technical properties. Basic motor drive circuitry. MEMS techonology. Actuators
Review of a.c. and d.c. motors. Brusless d.c. motor, d.c. servomotor. Stepper motor, example of stepper motor control circuitry. Motor selection. Synchros and resolvers. Pneumatic and hydraulic actuators.

Brusless d.c. motor, d.c. servomotor. Stepper motor, example of stepper motor control circuitry. Motor selection. Synchros and resolvers. Pneumatic and hydraulic actuators. Sensors
Motion, magnetic, capacitive, optical, ulrasonic, piezoelectric sensors and its industial application to measure different quantities such as distance, proximity, temperature, pressure, forces, torque, velocity acceleration, vibration, and etc. Gears
Types of gears. Nomenclatures. Involute properties. Spur gears. Helical gears. Bevel gears. Worm gears. Gear trains
Controllers
Introduction to programmable logic controllers (PLC), PLC instructions, timing and counting. Ladder logic programming. Electromechanical and solid state switches.

Worm gears. Gear trains
Controllers
Introduction to programmable logic controllers (PLC), PLC instructions, timing and counting. Ladder logic programming. Electromechanical and solid state switches. Course Learning Outcomes:

Subject Code:	ARR6143

Robotics - Modelling and Control
Subject Code:	ARR6143
Objective:	To provides students with rigorous mathematical skills on modelling and control of robotic arm and similar manipulator. Pre-Requisite:	None
Credit Hours:	3
Contact Hours:	44 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)
Assessment:	Test: 20%
Assignment: 20%
Lab Test: 20%
Final exam: 40%
Laboratory:	Control and automation lab, Robotic arms. Course Contents:
Representing Position and Orientation
Representing pose in 2D. Representing pose in 3D, combining translation and orientation. Robot Modeling
Modeling robotic arms. Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters. Link frame assignments. Robot manipulator modeling. Examples of robot link sequences. Forward and Inverse Kinematics
Forward kinematics: A 2-link robot. A 6-axis robot. Inverse kinematics: Closed-form solution. Numerical solution. Under-actuated manipulator. Redundant manipulator
Trajectory Generation
Joint-space motion. Cartesian motion.

A 6-axis robot. Inverse kinematics: Closed-form solution. Numerical solution. Under-actuated manipulator. Redundant manipulator
Trajectory Generation
Joint-space motion. Cartesian motion. Motion through a singularity. Configuration change. Velocity Relationships
Manipulator Jacobian. Resolved-rate motion control. Force relationship. A general numerical approach for inverse kinematics. Dynamic and Control
Equations of motions – gravity term, intertia matrix, coriolis matrix, payload, base force, dynamic manipulability. Drive train friction. Forward dynamics. Linear manipulator joint control.

Drive train friction. Forward dynamics. Linear manipulator joint control. Course Learning Outcomes:
At the completion of the subject, students should be able to perform the following tasks:
Course Learning Outcomes	Level of Emphasis
CLO1 – Compute the pose of a robotic arm given the configuration, links and angle of each joint. (cognitive – applying, level 3)	–
CLO2 – Deduce the joint angle of each robotic arm link given the required pose via inverse kinematic. (cognitive – evaluating, level 5)	–
CLO3 – Generate the trajectory for the end-effector of a robotic arm via joint-space or Cartesian space schemes using anticipatory thinking. (cognitive – creating, level 6)	–
CLO4 – Construct linear trajectory-following control scheme for robotic arm.  (psychomotor – mechanism, level 4)	–

Introduction to mobile robots and drones
Robots and embedded controllers, sensors, actuators, control, multitasking, communications. Wheeled robots

Course Contents:
Robot Programming Environment
Introduction to Robot Operating System (ROS) development environmen, tools and ecosystem. Modeling, Simulation and Visualization
Using ROS tools for 3D robot modeling, robotic arm and mobile robot simulation and visualization.

Modeling, Simulation and Visualization
Using ROS tools for 3D robot modeling, robotic arm and mobile robot simulation and visualization. Sensing, Vision and Depth
Perception of the world using sensors, cameras and depth cameras via 3D point cloud processing and object recognition
Kinematics and Control
Forward and inverse kinematics problem for serial manipulators. Create and tune Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controllers for system behaviour.

Create a Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) implementation using Kalman Filtering of noisy data, Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) and probabilistic occupancy grid mapping

Navigation with classic 2D and 3D path planning and improvement with Reinforcement Learning and SLAM
